name: chana masala
instructions:
The lighter, tangier chickpea preparation. Distinguished from chole by amchur + lemon-ZEST as the tang stack (no black tea, no lemon juice — see notes), more visible tomato, and welcome peppers. Onion form is flexible. ROSEMARY IS A SECRET INGREDIENT (see notes) — non-optional now.

SHARED SPICE BASE: cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, plus amchur-heavy tang finish and kasoori methi. NO cardamom in chana masala — that's chole territory.

CHANA MASALA-SPECIFIC SPICE ADDITIONS: ajwain (carom seeds), dried oregano, and dried thyme. Together with the rosemary at the finish, these push chana masala further into its mediterranean-adjacent register. Add ajwain to the whole-spice bloom (with the bay leaf); add oregano and thyme with the ground spices.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Chickpeas as the legume.
- ONIONS: FINELY DICED or PUREED preferred, but chana masala TOLERATES chunky whole onions (unlike chole's strict puree rule). Chunky onions are visible in the finished dish and read more rustic/homestyle.
- WHOLE SPICES in the bloom: bay leaf. NO cardamom.
- NO black tea (that's chole).
- FRESH TOMATOES (chopped or lightly pureed) OR CANNED TOMATOES — both work here as primary; more visible tomato presence than chole.
- Optional small addition of tomato paste for depth (secondary — not the tomato source itself).
- Ghee or vegetable oil both fine.
- AMCHUR POWDER is the tang slot — heavier than in the other family variants.
- LEMON ZEST at the finish. NO lemon juice (see notes).
- NO GREEN PEPPERS — see notes. This variant doesn't take them; heat comes from chili powder + kashmiri chili powder + amchur tang instead.
- ROSEMARY, cilantro, and mint all at the finish — the herb trio. Kasoori methi too but subordinate.
- NO cinnamon, NO cloves, NO cardamom — those are chole territory (see chole-masala).

PROCEDURE:
1. Heat ghee or oil. Bloom bay leaf and ajwain briefly.
2. Add onions (finely diced or pureed; chunky tolerated). Cook until soft.
3. Add ginger and garlic. Sauté briefly. (NO fresh green peppers — see notes.)
4. Add ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, amchur, chili powder) plus dried oregano and dried thyme. Toast in the fat.
5. Add chopped or lightly pureed fresh tomatoes (or canned). Cook down until the fat separates. Small spoon of tomato paste optional here for depth.
6. Add chickpeas (canned or pre-cooked) and enough water to loosen. Simmer to integrate.
7. Finish OFF HEAT with lemon zest (NOT juice), kasoori methi crumbled in, chopped cilantro, torn mint leaves, and a generous scatter of fresh rosemary.
notes:
This recipe used to be Variant B of a master-tomato-dal file. Now standalone.

CHOLE VS CHANA MASALA — WHY THEY'RE SEPARATE RECIPES: chole (see chole-masala) and chana masala are both chickpea dishes but represent genuinely different preparations. Chole leans darker, thicker, more whole-spice-and-tea forward — black tea, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, sometimes anardana for tartness. Chana masala (this recipe) leans lighter, tangier, and amchur/lemon-zest-forward, skips the tea, more visible tomato, peppers welcome, mint and rosemary at the finish. In indian home cooking these are often blurred, but the axes above are the real distinction. Pick one direction; don't try to hedge.

CHOLE AND CHANA ALSO REFER TO DIFFERENT CHICKPEA VARIETIES: kabuli chana (large beige, western-supermarket default) vs kala chana (small dark brown/black, indian-grocery specialty). Each has its own preferred dish style. Recipe defaults to kabuli chana.

=== AJWAIN + OREGANO + THYME — THE MEDITERRANEAN LEAN ===

Ajwain (carom seeds) belongs in the whole-spice bloom alongside the bay leaf. Its thyme-and-oregano-adjacent character with a slight bitter bite pairs particularly well with chickpeas and reinforces the mediterranean-adjacent identity chana masala leans on. Dried oregano and dried thyme go in with the ground spices — together they add a herbaceous background that makes the rosemary-at-the-finish (see below) land more cleanly. Without this trio, the rosemary can read like an outlier addition; with them, the whole herb axis feels intentional.

=== ROSEMARY IS THE SECRET INGREDIENT ===

Rosemary is so good in chana masala I now consider it the secret ingredient of the recipe. Previously listed as want-to-try; the test batch confirmed it. The logic: chana masala's lemon-forward, tomato-visible, peppers-and-mint character is closer to a mediterranean-adjacent indian register than the darker chole. Rosemary's piney depth pairs cleanly with the amchur-and-lemon-zest axis and adds a second-dimension herb note the way it does in mediterranean tomato dishes. Scatter fresh rosemary generously off heat with the cilantro and mint. Non-optional now — the dish reads noticeably flatter without it. Don't tell anyone this is chana masala's secret; let them think it's the amchur.

=== TANG STACK — AMCHUR + LEMON ZEST (NOT LEMON JUICE) ===

AMCHUR AND LEMON ZEST ARE THE TANG STACK — LEMON JUICE IS CLASHING: chana masala's brightness comes from amchur (dried mango powder) plus lemon zest ONLY. Lemon juice is a departure from this recipe. Reasoning: lemon zest concentrates the citrus oils without adding water; juice adds water and thins the sauce, and its rounder acid pushes the dish toward a lemon-butter/mediterranean register that fights the earthy amchur tang. Zest carries the brightness without diluting anything. Amchur is the earthy-sweet-tart complement to the citrus oils. Together they define the chana-masala-specific character that separates this from chole.

=== TOMATO FORM — FRESH OR CANNED (BOTH PRIMARY), PASTE IS SECONDARY ===

Unlike chole (fresh only) and rajma (paste only), chana masala is flexible on tomato form. Fresh tomatoes AND canned tomatoes are both primary here — pick whichever fits your pantry and mood. Fresh reads brighter and slightly less concentrated; canned reads deeper and more integrated. Tomato paste is welcome as a SECONDARY addition — a small spoonful cooked into the base for depth alongside the fresh/canned tomatoes. Paste alone (no fresh/canned) is rajma territory — don't go there.

=== NO GREEN PEPPERS ===

Green peppers (poblano, jalapeño, serrano, anaheim, bell) are all wrong in chana masala. Chole-masala and mediterranean-dal are the two family variants that take green peppers; chana masala and rajma don't. Reasoning: chana masala's flavor structure is amchur + lemon zest + rosemary + mint on a fresh/canned tomato base — a tangy, herbal, mediterranean-adjacent register. Green peppers push the dish toward homestyle-curry territory (mediterranean dal's slot) and fight the rosemary-and-lemon-zest identity. Heat comes from chili powder + kashmiri chili powder + amchur instead — dry, warm, and tang-forward rather than vegetal-green.

=== MINT AT THE FINISH ===

Mint stirred in raw at the end (with the cilantro and rosemary) works well in chana masala for the same heavy-spice-cabinet reason it works in chole — the spice stack absorbs mint's edge. Don't cook it in.

=== NO CINNAMON, NO CLOVES, NO CARDAMOM ===

All three are chole territory. Adding them here pushes chana masala toward chole and loses the lighter tangier identity. Cardamom is the sneakiest — it's the whole spice most people default to for indian bloom-in-oil work, but it's specifically wrong for chana masala. The floral cardamom character fights the rosemary + amchur + lemon zest trio and pushes the dish back toward chole. If you find yourself reaching for any of these, make chole-masala instead.

=== SHARED FAMILY RULES ===

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: color and gentle heat without overpowering. Different from generic chili powder. Non-optional across the tomato-dal family (chole, chana masala, mediterranean dal, rajma).

KASOORI METHI: non-optional in this family. Load-bearing flavor, not a garnish.

SIBLING RECIPES:
- chole-masala: the darker, thicker, more aromatic chickpea preparation with black tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon.
- mediterranean-dal: the chunky lentil dal — dry lentils into the pot, lots of chunky peppers and onions. The most 'curry' of the family.
- rajma: the kidney bean gravy — tomato paste concentrated, ghee non-optional, methi-forward.
